findings.description: Assembly loop range(total_chunks - 1) silently drops the final chunk, so every multi-chunk file is reassembled truncated despite is_complete() confirming all chunks arrived.

dest_path joins UPLOAD_DIR with the client-supplied filename, so a name like '../../etc/cron.d/evil.pdf' passes the extension check yet writes outside the upload directory (path traversal / arbitrary file write).
Chunk paths are built from the untrusted upload_id with no sanitization, allowing traversal/overwrite of arbitrary files via crafted upload ids in receive_chunk and cleanup.
verify_checksum compares the stored file against a SHA-256 supplied by the same untrusted client that sent the bytes, so it validates nothing; it is also never called anywhere in the flow.
MAX_FILE_MB / file_size_ok are declared but never enforced; receive_chunk writes unbounded data for unbounded indices, enabling an unbounded disk-fill DoS.
verify_checksum reads the entire (potentially 200MB+) file into memory with f.read() instead of streaming the hash in chunks, risking memory exhaustion.

_sessions dict and chunk files are mutated across receive_chunk/assemble/abort with no locking, so concurrent chunks for one id race and two uploads sharing a filename clobber each other's destination.
open()/write() in receive_chunk has no error handling, so a disk-full or permission error raises uncaught instead of degrading gracefully.
start() overwrites any existing session for the same upload_id, silently discarding an in-progress upload's received set with no idempotency guard.

is_complete trusts only the count of received indices; a duplicate write to the same index is deduplicated by the set, but a missing-then-extra scenario cannot occur, yet completeness is never cross-checked against on-disk chunk presence.
